jsonLdWeb.name: Plywood Calculator
jsonLdWeb.description: Calculate plywood sheets needed for any project with waste factor and cost estimate.

mainEntity.name: How many square feet is a sheet of plywood?

acceptedAnswer.text: A standard 4×8 foot sheet of plywood covers 32 square feet. Other common sizes: 4×4 (16 sq ft), 4×10 (40 sq ft), and 4×12 (48 sq ft). To find sheets needed, divide your total area by 32 and add waste factor.

mainEntity.name: How do I calculate plywood needed?

acceptedAnswer.text: Sheets = (Length × Width) ÷ 32 × (1 + waste%). For a 12×20 ft floor: 240 ÷ 32 = 7.5, with 10% waste: 7.5 × 1.10 = 8.25, round up to 9 sheets. For walls, calculate each wall's area, add them up, subtract openings, and divide by 32.

mainEntity.name: What thickness of plywood do I need?

acceptedAnswer.text: Common thicknesses and uses: 1/4" for underlayment and backing, 3/8" for wall sheathing (non-structural), 1/2" for wall sheathing and cabinets, 5/8" for roof sheathing, and 3/4" for subfloor and structural applications. Subfloor plywood on 16" OC joists typically requires 3/4" (23/32") tongue-and-groove.

mainEntity.name: How much does a sheet of plywood cost?

acceptedAnswer.text: Prices vary by thickness, grade, and type. Approximate 4×8 sheet prices: 1/4" CDX $15-25, 1/2" CDX $25-40, 3/4" CDX $35-55, 3/4" sanded BC $50-70, 3/4" birch plywood $55-85, and 3/4" marine grade $80-120. Prices fluctuate with lumber markets.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between CDX and sanded plywood?

acceptedAnswer.text: CDX is construction grade with C-grade front, D-grade back, and X for exterior glue. It is rough, with knots and patches, used for sheathing, subfloor, and roofing. Sanded (BC, AB) plywood has smooth faces suitable for cabinets, furniture, and visible surfaces. Both are available in the same thicknesses.

mainEntity.name: How much waste should I plan for?

acceptedAnswer.text: Plan for 10% waste on simple rectangular projects (floors, roofs). Add 15% for projects with angles and cutouts (walls with windows). Use 20% for complex shapes. If using plywood for irregular shapes or requiring grain matching, plan for 20-25% waste.

howItWorks:
How to Calculate Plywood Sheets

Plywood calculations are straightforward: divide the total area by the sheet size, then add a waste factor for cuts and fitting.

Sheets = (Area ÷ 32) × (1 + Waste %)
  • Total area = length × width (in feet)
  • A standard 4×8 sheet covers 32 square feet
  • Divide area by 32 to get the minimum number of sheets
  • Add 10% waste for simple layouts, 15-20% for complex
  • Always round up to the next whole sheet
  • For multiple rooms or walls, add all areas together first
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

How many square feet is a sheet of plywood?

A standard 4×8 sheet covers 32 square feet. Also available: 4×10 (40 sq ft) and 4×12 (48 sq ft). Divide your total area by the sheet size to find how many sheets you need.

How do I calculate plywood needed?

Sheets = (Length × Width) ÷ 32 × (1 + waste%). For a 12×20 ft floor: 240 ÷ 32 = 7.5, with 10% waste = 8.25, round up to 9 sheets.

What thickness do I need?

1/4" for underlayment, 3/8" for non-structural sheathing, 1/2" for wall sheathing, 5/8" for roof sheathing, 3/4" for subfloor and structural. Check code for your specific application.

How much does plywood cost?

4×8 CDX sheet prices: 1/4" $15-25, 1/2" $25-40, 3/4" $35-55. Sanded/cabinet grade costs 30-50% more. Prices fluctuate with lumber markets.

CDX vs sanded plywood?

CDX has rough faces (C and D grade) with exterior glue — used for sheathing, subfloor, and roofing. Sanded plywood (BC, AB grades) has smooth faces for cabinets, furniture, and visible surfaces.

How much waste to plan for?

10% for rectangular areas, 15% for walls with windows/doors, 20% for complex shapes or grain-matched projects.

howWeCalculate.intro: The math behind a plywood count is short: turn your surface into square feet, divide by the square footage of one sheet, pad for cutoffs, then round up because a lumberyard sells whole sheets.
steps: Multiply your area length by width to get total square feet — a 12 × 16 ft floor is 192 sq ft.
Divide that area by the square footage of your chosen sheet: 32 sq ft for a 4 × 8, 40 for a 4 × 10, 48 for a 4 × 12. 192 ÷ 32 = 6 sheets before waste.
Multiply by 1 plus your waste factor to cover offcuts and mistakes: 6 × 1.10 ≈ 6.6.
Round up to the next whole sheet — 6.6 becomes 7. You buy 7 sheets for that 12 × 16 floor.
howWeCalculate.disclaimer: This gives you a purchase count, not a cut list. Odd rooms, staggered seams over joists, and grain direction can push the real number a sheet or two higher, so confirm against your actual layout before you buy.

whenToUse.intro: Reach for this whenever you're covering a flat area with 4-foot-wide sheet goods and need to know how many to load in the truck.
scenarios: Sizing subfloor: 3/4" tongue-and-groove over floor joists, where sheets run perpendicular to the joists with staggered end seams.
Ordering wall sheathing: 1/2" or 7/16" panels over studs, then subtracting window and door openings from the wall area.
Counting roof decking: 5/8" sheathing across the rafters, remembering that a sloped roof has more area than its floor footprint.
Estimating a shed, deck box, or workbench top where you just need whole sheets covering a known rectangle.
Checking a lumberyard or contractor's sheet count before you pay for the order.

sections.title: One sheet, 32 square feet
sections.body: A standard 4 × 8 ft plywood sheet covers 32 square feet — that single number does most of the work. Take a 12 × 16 ft floor: that's 192 sq ft, and at 32 sq ft per sheet that's 6 sheets exactly. Add roughly 10% for the pieces you cut off and can't reuse and you land at 7 sheets to buy. The calculator runs that same chain for any dimensions and lets you swap in 4 × 10 (40 sq ft) or 4 × 12 (48 sq ft) panels if your supplier stocks them.

sections.title: Why you round up, and why you pad for waste
sections.body: Lumberyards sell whole sheets, so a result of 6.6 sheets means you carry home 7 — there's no such thing as buying two-thirds of a panel. The waste factor exists because real layouts never divide cleanly: you rip a strip off one sheet to close a gap and the leftover is too small for the next run. Ten percent covers straight rectangular work like a floor or roof; bump it to 15% once a wall is full of window and door openings you have to cut around, or 20% for angled and irregular shapes.

sections.title: Match the thickness to the job
sections.body: Sheet count and thickness are separate decisions. Subfloor over joists at 16" on-center is usually 3/4" (nominal 23/32") tongue-and-groove; exterior wall sheathing runs 1/2" or 7/16"; roof decking is commonly 5/8". Thinner 1/4" and 3/8" panels are for underlayment and backing, not structure. The area math is identical whatever thickness you pick — thickness drives the weight per sheet and the price, not how many you need to cover the surface.

howToSteps.title: Measure the area in feet
howToSteps.description: Enter the length and width of the surface you're covering. For a floor or roof deck, that's the plan dimensions; for a wall, use its length and height.

howToSteps.title: Pick your sheet size
howToSteps.description: Leave it on 4 × 8 (32 sq ft) unless your yard stocks the longer 4 × 10 or 4 × 12 panels, which cut down on seams for large spans.

howToSteps.title: Set thickness and waste
howToSteps.description: Choose the thickness for your application, then a waste factor: 10% for open rectangles, 15% for walls with openings, 20% for complex cuts. Add a price per sheet if you want a cost total.

howToSteps.title: Read the sheet count
howToSteps.description: The result rounds up to whole sheets and shows the coverage those sheets provide including waste. That's your shopping number.

tips: Order at least one extra full sheet beyond the count for the sheet you'll inevitably cut wrong or find damaged when you open the bundle.
On a subfloor, plan to stagger the end joints so seams don't line up across joists — that staggering is part of what the waste factor pays for.
A pitched roof has more surface than its footprint: a 6/12 roof is about 12% larger than the floor below it, so measure along the slope, not the ceiling.
Subtract big openings before you divide: a 10 × 8 ft wall is 80 sq ft, but a 3 × 5 window and a 3 × 7 door remove 36 sq ft, leaving 44 to sheathe.
Buy full sheets rather than pre-cut halves when you can — 4 × 8 panels almost always cost less per square foot than 2 × 4 or 4 × 4 pieces.

faq.question: How many square feet is a sheet of plywood?
faq.answer: A standard 4 × 8 ft sheet covers 32 square feet (4 × 8 = 32). A 4 × 10 sheet is 40 sq ft and a 4 × 12 is 48 sq ft. To count sheets, divide your total area by that number and round up.

faq.question: How much waste should I add?
faq.answer: About 10% for simple rectangular areas like a floor or roof deck, 15% for walls with windows and doors you cut around, and 20% for angled or irregular shapes. The waste covers offcut strips that are too small to reuse.

faq.question: Do I round up or down on the sheet count?
faq.answer: Always up. Yards sell whole sheets, so a calculated 6.6 sheets means you buy 7. Rounding down leaves you short mid-project. This calculator rounds up for you automatically.

faq.question: What's the difference between subfloor and sheathing plywood?
faq.answer: Subfloor is the structural layer over your floor joists, typically 3/4" tongue-and-groove that carries foot traffic and furniture. Sheathing is the panel skin over wall studs or roof rafters, usually thinner (7/16" to 5/8") and often CDX with exterior glue. The sheet-count math is the same for both; only the thickness and grade change.

faq.question: How do I figure sheets for a whole floor?
faq.answer: Multiply length by width for the area, divide by 32, then add waste and round up. A 12 × 16 ft room is 192 sq ft; 192 ÷ 32 = 6 sheets, or 7 once you add 10% for cuts. For an L-shaped or multi-room floor, add each rectangle's area together before dividing.

faq.question: Does thickness change how many sheets I need?
faq.answer: No. A 4 × 8 sheet covers 32 sq ft whether it's 1/4" or 3/4", so the count stays the same. Thickness only changes the weight per sheet and the price — a 3/4" sheet weighs about 60 lbs versus roughly 22 lbs for 1/4".
